segments.s232-g265: فریبرز گفت is subject + past-tense verb (SOV order is normal in Persian poetry here): گفت is the simple past third-person singular 'said'. ای + هژبر ژیان is a vocative phrase: ای is the exclamation 'O!', followed by the name هژبر and the epithet ژیان ('life' or an affectionate epithet) placed after the name as an appositive. The vocative addresses Hejbar directly and links to what follows as direct speech.
segments.s232-g266: منم راه را تنگ بسته میان is literal word order: منم (I‑am / I am the one) provides the subject/auxiliary; راه را marks 'the road' as a definite object (را is the object marker). تنگ بسته is a compound describing the state of the road ('narrow/closely shut' — بسته is a past participle 'closed' used adjectivally with تنگ 'narrow/tightly'), and میان is an adverb/prepositional element 'between' or 'in the middle'. Poetic/archaic word order places the copular element with the pronoun (منم) separated from the participle (بسته) instead of the more colloquial بسته‌ام.
segments.s232-g49: A series of noun phrases in apposition: 'تن' (the body) + a prepositional phrase 'از جنگ' (from war) qualifies it, then a second coordinated noun phrase 'خسته‌دل' (lit. 'wearied-heart') + 'از رزم' (from battle) + adjective 'شاد' (joyful). Word order is poetic: descriptors follow the nouns they modify, and both parts juxtapose physical exhaustion with inner exhilaration.
segments.s232-g50: 'جهان را' uses the object/definiteness marker 'را' to topicalize 'the world'; 'چنینست' is the fixed copular statement 'such is / thus is'. The nouns 'ساز و نهاد' form a binomial (joined by 'و') meaning the world's arrangement/constitution — both nouns describe what 'the world' is like.
segments.s232-g51: 'پر از خون' is an adjectival phrase 'full of blood' (پر + از + noun). What follows lists items 'بر و تیغ و پای و رکیب' linked by 'و' (and): 'بر' here 'upon/on' (registering where blood is), 'تیغ' (sword/blade), 'پای' (foot/hoof), 'رکیب' (rival/opponent). The coordination and elided copula are typical of epic poetic compression.
segments.s232-g52: Begins with poetic preposition 'ز' (= از) 'from'; 'کشته' is the slain (noun), 'پیدا' 'visible/seen'. 'فراز از نشیب' is a frozen pair meaning 'from high to low' or 'on summit and descent', with the second 'از' again expressing 'from'. The clause states that among the slain nothing distinguishable/visible (pیدا) remained across heights and depths.
segments.s232-g53: 'چنین' ('thus') starts the clause; 'تا' here means 'until'. 'بشستن' is the infinitive 'to wash' (used as the complement), and 'نپرداختند' is the past negative plural of 'پرداختن' (here 'to attend to/finish'); together the phrase means 'until they had not yet finished (the) washing' — a classical, somewhat inverted way to express 'until they finished washing'.
segments.s232-g54: Literal order 'یک از دیگری باز نشناختند' = 'one from the other they again did not recognize'. 'یک از دیگری' = 'one another/each other'; 'باز' (here) nuances 'anymore/again' and 'نشناختند' is past negative 'they did not recognize'. The phrase means they ceased to recognize one another.
segments.s232-g55: 'سر و تن بشستند' is a simple past plural 'they washed head and body' (compound object coordinated by 'و'). 'و دل شسته بود' is a compound perfect/pluperfect: 'and the heart had been washed' — 'شسته بود' is past participle + auxiliary 'بود' indicating a prior or completed state.
segments.s232-g56: 'که' introduces an explanatory clause 'that / for'; 'دشمن' is subject 'the enemy'; 'ببند' is the root of 'بستن' (here used as the verbal idea 'to bind/fasten') and combines with the following participial/adjectival 'گران بسته بود' ('had fastened heavy [bonds]') — the whole clause means the enemy had made heavy closures/bonds. The ordering and duplication of binding verbs is a poetic register common in classical Persian.
segments.s232-g65: چنین (adverb) 'thus/so' comes first to set the manner; گفت is the past-tense verb 'said' (poetic V‑initial order: 'Thus said Rostam'). رستم is the subject after the verb. بگودرز uses the prepositional prefix ب (بِ-) attached to the name to mean 'to Goudarz' (i.e. 'said to Goudarz'), and و گیو simply adds 'and Giv' as another indirect object/recipient.
segments.s232-g66: بدان here is composed of the prepositional prefix ب (be-, 'to') + دان (functioning as a demonstrative/compound 'that/those' in poetic usage) and yields 'to those' or 'to that company'; نامداران is a plural noun 'the renowned/illustrious ones'; و links the following noun; گردان is 'troop/company' and نیو is a following name/epithet attached to گردان (here treated as the troop's name or qualifier). Word order is noun phrase after the verb phrase, completing the indirect‑object/recipient idea begun in the previous segment.
segments.s232-g67: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'; آگاهی here means 'information/news' (an object of آمد); آمد is the past 'came' (perfective); بشاه is the prepositional phrase 'to the king' (بِ + شاه) and جهان modifies شاه as 'king of the world' (a title). The order is subordinate clause (temporal) + clause.
segments.s232-g68: بمن is the prepositional phrase 'to me'; باز here means 'again' (modify گفت); گفت is the past 'said/told' (verb) — the provided lexical senses list did not include the verbal sense, so marked accordingly; این سخن is 'this speech/word' (direct object); در نهان is a locative phrase 'in secret' (در + نهان). The clause means 'he again told me this in private.'
segments.s232-g69: که is the subordinator 'that' (introducing reported content). طوس (Tūs) is the personal name (here the hero, not the city) followed by سپهبد 'spahbed/general' as an apposition/title. بکوه is بِ + کوه 'to the mountain' (directional), and آمدست is an archaic/perfect past 'has come' (compound past form common in epic Persian). Word order reflects reported statement.
segments.s232-g70: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'; پیران is plural 'the elders' (source); و links the next source; هومان (Houmān) is a proper name; ستوه is an adjective 'weary/sick of' describing the subject; آمدست repeats the perfective 'has come.' The clause literally explains cause/origin and the condition in which he arrived: 'he came from the elders and Houmān, weary.'
segments.s232-g71: از ایران برفتیم is 'we went away from Iran' (از = from; برفتیم past 'we departed'); با رای و هوش is an instrumental phrase 'with counsel/plan and wit/intellect' (با + two coordinating nouns). The verb is plural past, matching the first-person plural subject implied by برفتیم.
segments.s232-g72: برآمد is a past/Perfective verb 'arose/issued forth'; ز پیکار 'from the battle' gives the source; مغزم 'my brain' is the subject (مغز + enclitic ـم = 'my brain'); بجوش is a present/imperative-form root meaning 'boil, seethe' used here poetically to mean 'seethed/was in turmoil' — together the clause says 'from the battle my brain rose up, seething.'
segments.s232-g153: ندیدند is a 3rd-person plural past verb 'they did not see.' The negative applies to کسی ('anyone/no one'); کسی را uses the classical object/topic marker را even though the clause is negative, yielding the sense 'they saw no one.' زنده precedes کسی as an adjective 'alive.' بجای begins the next phrase and means 'in place of' (introducing what has been put in the place of something lost).
segments.s232-g154: This is a verbless nominal phrase describing what exists 'in place of' the expected settlement. زمین 'ground/earth' is the first item; بود is the past copula 'there was.' The list continues with و 'and' joining خرگاه (a lowly stable/donkey-stall) and پرده‌سرای (a curtained chamber/room). The overall structure is poetic parataxis: noun — copula — noun, noun.
segments.s232-g155: بنزدیک is an adverbial compound 'nearby/close at hand' placed before the subject. رستم is the proper-name subject; رسید is the simple past 'reached' (here 'news reached' or 'he received news'). آگهی is the noun 'news/information' (object of رسید, understood: 'information reached Rostam').
segments.s232-g156: که introduces a subordinate clause 'that...'. شد is the past of شدن 'became' (here 'became/was made'); روی کشور is a genitival phrase 'the face/surface of the country' (روی = face/surface, کشور = country). ز + ترکان = 'from the Turks' (classical preposition), and تهی 'empty/devoid' predicates the phrase: 'the country's face became empty of the Turks.'
segments.s232-g157: ز here introduces cause 'because of / from.' ناباکی is an abstract noun 'wickedness/treachery' (the moral failing that provoked him). و links another cause, خواب 'sleep' meaning 'slumber/inactivity,' followed by ایرانیان 'the Iranians' as the possessors/agents of that sleep — classical word order lists causes before the verb of reaction.
segments.s232-g158: برآشفت is a past simple verb 'he became angry / sprang up in anger' (compound with the prefix بر- for sudden agitation). رستم is the subject; چو is the comparative 'like/as.' شیر ژیان is a poetic simile: شیر 'lion' + ژیان (literally 'life') here functioning adjectivally to mean 'living' or 'lively' — 'like a living lion.'
segments.s232-g159: زبان را uses را as a topical/object marker — 'his tongue' (object/topic of the verb). بدشنام is an adjective modifying زبان, 'ill-famed' or 'slanderous,' so زبان را بدشنام بگشاد is an idiom meaning 'he opened his slanderous tongue' (bگشاد here is past 'opened' with the causative/past prefix b- in classical style). و گفت follows as 'and said' introducing direct speech.
segments.s232-g160: که introduces the content clause 'that...'. کس را here uses را after کس for emphasis/definiteness in classical Persian; with the negative predicate خرد نیست ('there is no wisdom') the sense is 'no one has wisdom.' با introduces the prepositional phrase 'with' and مغز جفت literally 'paired brain' — an idiom meaning a properly functioning or matching intellect; the whole clause asserts that nobody's intellect is properly matched to their brain (i.e., nobody is wise).
segments.s232-g145: بباید is a modal (older/emphatic form of باید) meaning 'it must / one must'. It is followed by the infinitive شدن ('to become / to go' here functioning as the action required). سوی آن رزمگاه is a prepositional phrase 'toward that battlefield' (سوی = toward, آن = that, رزمگاه = battlefield); the order (modal + infinitive + goal) is characteristic of older/poetic Persian.
segments.s232-g146: This line shows poetic inversion. The normal prose order would be سپاه باید بهر سو فرستاد ('the army must be sent to that side'), but here فرستاد (sent/dispatch) is placed early for rhythm. بهر سو is an adverbial phrase 'to/ toward that side', باید is the modal 'must', and سپاه is the subject 'army' placed at the end for emphasis.
segments.s232-g147: شد here is the simple past/intransitive 'went/advanced' (an older usage of شدن). از پیش او = 'from before him / ahead of him' (showing motion in front of another). بیژن شیر مرد is an appositive: the name (بیژن) followed by an epithet 'شیر مرد' ('lion-man' = valiant man). The sequence verb → adverbial → agent/epithet is typical of epic word order.
segments.s232-g148: بجایی کجا is a poetic way of saying 'to the place where…' (بجایی literally 'to a place', followed by the interrogative/relative کجا 'where'). بود is the past-tense copula 'was', and دشت نبرد is a compound 'plain of battle' (دشت = plain, نبرد = battle). Together: 'to the place where the battlefield/plain lay.'
segments.s232-g149: جهان دید is 'he saw the world' (noun before verb; poetic ordering). پر + کشته = 'full of the slain' (پر از X = full of X). و خواسته follows as a coordinate; خواسته here acts as an adjective/participial noun 'the bereaved/one who is in need/desiring' (older/poetic nuance), completing the image of many affected people. The whole phrase describes what he beheld.
segments.s232-g150: بهر سو is the directional phrase 'toward that side'. نشستی here is the verb (poetic past) meaning 'he sat' (the provided lemma senses list is mismatched; the intended sense is 'to sit'), and بیاراسته is an adjective 'unadorned / dishevelled / not made ready', describing his appearance or posture as he sat facing that side. The verb follows the adverbial for emphasis.
segments.s232-g151: پراگنده کشور is 'the widespread / scattered country' (adjective before noun). پر از خسته = 'full of the weary' (پر از = full of; خسته = weary/tired). دید is the past 'he saw'. The sequence describes the extent of suffering he observed across the land.
segments.s232-g152: بخاک اندر افگنده is a verbal phrase 'cast/thrown to the ground' (بخاک = to the ground; اندر = in/into; افگنده = cast/laid). پا بسته is a compact description 'feet bound' (پا = foot/feet; بسته = bound/tied). دید closes the clause: 'he saw (them) thrown to the ground, feet bound.'
segments.s232-g177: که introduces a clause meaning 'that' or 'for.' پیروز is a predicative adjective placed before the verb (poetic word order) meaning 'victorious.' برگشتم is a past‑tense first‑person verb 'I returned,' and از کارزار is a prepositional phrase 'from the battlefield' that completes the verb.
segments.s232-g178: تبه شد is a compact clause meaning roughly 'misfortune/harm happened' (تبه = mischief/ruin; شد here = 'happened'). نکو گشته is a perfect/resultative phrase 'has become good' (گشته as perfect participle), and فرجام کار is the noun phrase 'the outcome/end of the affair' with the modifier order reversed for poetic effect.
segments.s232-g179: برآشفت is a past verb meaning 'he became angry/was enraged.' با + طوس indicates the object/target 'with/against Tous' (طوس here the person). و شد 'and he became' links to the simile چون پلنگ 'like a leopard.'
segments.s232-g180: که here gives a causal/explanatory sense 'for' or 'that.' این جای خوابست‌گر is a compressed, poetic description meaning roughly 'this is a sleep‑inducing place / a place for sleep' (خوابست‌گر as a poetic/syncretic form). دشت جنگ places the contrast 'the plain is for battle,' with دشت = 'plain' and جنگ = 'battle.'
segments.s232-g181: طلایه نگه کن is an imperative phrase 'keep watch on the vanguard' (نگه + کن = 'keep/look'). که introduces the indirect question 'who is from the host' (از خیل = 'of/from the host'); کیست is the fused interrogative 'who is it' (کی + ست).
segments.s232-g182: سرآهنگ means 'the chief/leader' (literally 'head‑opening' used as 'leader') and آن دوده را is the topicalized object 'that dust/smoke' (را marks it). نام چیست is the question 'what is (his) name?' with چیست = چی + ست 'what is.'
segments.s232-g183: چو introduces a temporal/conditional 'when/if.' مرد طلایه 'a man of the vanguard' is the object; بیابی is a subjunctive/imperative‑flavored form of 'to find' (here giving the conditional sense 'if/when you find'), and بچوب is an imperative 'strike (with a stick).'
segments.s232-g184: هم اندر زمان 'at the same time / in that moment' (هم = also/at once; اندر زمان = in the moment). دست و پایش is the possessed plural phrase 'his hands and his feet' (ش is the possessive suffix attached to پای), and بکوب is the imperative 'strike/beat.'
segments.s232-g105: کنون is an adverb meaning “now.” جامه رزم is a noun phrase where جامه (garment) is qualified by رزم (of battle) — together “battle‑garment(s).” بیرون here is an adverb/prepositional element “out/away,” and کنید is the plural/imperfective imperative or hortative of کردن (“do/make”) used as a command: “take out/bring forth.” Word order places the object phrase before the verbal imperative, typical of Persian commands.
segments.s232-g106: بآسایش is an adverbial phrase “with ease/comfortably.” آرایش is a noun “adornment/array,” and افزون is a postpositive qualifier meaning “more/more abundant.” کنید is again the plural imperative of کردن “do/make.” The whole segment is another hortatory clause telling the addressees to augment their adornment, with the manner expressed first (بآسایش).
segments.s232-g107: غم و کام دل is a coordinated noun phrase: غم (sorrow) and کام (desire) governed by دل (“of the heart”). بی‌گمان is an adverb “undoubtedly,” and بگذرد is the present/subjunctive form of گذشتن used here with future sense “will pass.” The clause asserts that these emotional states will pass.
segments.s232-g108: زمانه is personified as the subject (here meaning fortune/the world). دم ما is an object/genitive phrase “our moment/our breath.” همی is the old literary continuous/present particle that accompanies the verb, and بشمرد is present/subjunctive of شمردن “to count/consider.” Together: “Fortune counts/marks our moment.”
segments.s232-g109: همان به که is an idiomatic construction meaning “it is best/appropriate that…” (همان = “that/this fitting,” به = prepositional particle here introducing the clause). ما is the subject “we,” جام is the direct object “cup,” and می‌بشمریم is the present/habitual form “we count/measure/number.” The verb is prefixed with می- (continuous/present), forming the hortatory suggestion “we should count/measure the cup.”
segments.s232-g110: بدین is the fused prepositional phrase (ب + این) meaning “at/by/with this.” چرخ is the noun “wheel/sky/fate,” here personified as fate, and نامهربان is the adjective “unkind.” ننگریم is the negative present/subjunctive (ن‑ + بی‌نهایتive stem نگر + ـیم) meaning roughly “let us not look/regard.” The clause issues a hortatory negative: “let us not regard this unkind fate.”
segments.s232-g111: سپاس is a noun “thanks/praise.” از is the preposition “from/of/for” introducing the beneficiary or source, and جهاندار پیروزگر is a compound/title where جهاندار (“world‑holder/sovereign”) is qualified by پیروزگر (“victorious/triumphant”). The phrase is a short formula of praise: “thanks to/for the victorious sovereign.”
segments.s232-g112: کزویست is an archaic fused clause (که ز وی است = “which/from him is” or “that comes from him”). It introduces a list of attributes: مردی و بخت و هنر — three coordinated nouns. The structure says these qualities originate from (or belong to) that sovereign: “from him are manliness, fortune, and skill.”
segments.s232-g121: پسندیده باد is an optative phrase: پسندیده (adjective) + باد (optative particle) = “may ... be pleasing/approved.” این نژاد و گهر is the thing wished for (this lineage and jewel); word order is adjective/particle + noun phrase, so the optative governs the whole NP.
segments.s232-g122: هم آن بوم کو چون تو آرد ببر is a relative clause modifying 'آن بوم' (that homeland). کو is the archaic relative 'که' = 'which/that'; چون تو = 'like you'; آرد is the present/subjunctive/relative verb 'brings'; ببر is the noun 'tiger' as the object. هم here = 'also/too', linking this clause to the wish before it.
segments.s232-g123: تو دانی is present 'you know' (دانی). که introduces the content clause 'what you did to us': با ما = 'to/with us', چه کردی = 'what you did'. بمهر is a prepositional/adverbial compound 'بِ + مهر' meaning 'with love' or 'out of affection' and qualifies the action کردن.
segments.s232-g124: که here functions causally 'so that' / 'because': 'که از جان تو شاد بادا سپهر' = optative clause introduced by بادا ('may'). از جان تو = 'because of/from your life/being'; شاد = 'happy'; بادا = optative 'may'; سپهر = 'heavens' (the entity being wished to be glad).
segments.s232-g125: همه مرده بودیم = 'we had all been dead' — مرده used predicatively and بودیم is past-tense plural 'we were/ had been'. و برگشته روز is an adjectival/temporal phrase 'and the day having returned' (برگشته a perfect-participle form modifying روز).
segments.s232-g126: بتو is the prepositional phrase بِ + تو meaning 'because of you / by you / to you'; زنده گشتیم is a compound meaning 'we became alive' (زنده = alive, گشتیم = became, past plural); و گیتی‌فروز is an epithet appended by 'and' — گیتی‌فروز = 'world‑illuminating', describing the brightening effect.
segments.s232-g127: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past 'he ordered/said'. تا here introduces a purpose/subordinate clause ('that ...'), so تا + verb-clause = 'that they (should)...'. پیل = 'elephant'; با تخت عاج = 'with an ivory throne' (با = 'with', تخت = 'throne', عاج = 'ivory').
segments.s232-g128: بیارند is the subjunctive/volitive 3rd‑person plural of آوردن used in a commanded clause ('that they bring'); it follows the preceding تا. با طوق زرین و تاج = 'with a golden collar and a crown' (با = 'with', طوق = 'collar/necklace', زرین = 'golden', تاج = 'crown'). Note: the word-list senses for بیارند did not match the verbal use, so its sense is marked below accordingly.
segments.s232-g201: This is a coordinated noun phrase: کمرهای زرین (literally “the belts/waists of gold”) + و + بیجاده تاج (“and the بیجاده crown”). Adjectives follow the noun as usual in Persian poetry; کمرهای is plural ‘belts/girdles’. The conjunction و links two parallel items. The phrase omits an explicit ezāfe marker but functions as a simple list of ornaments.
segments.s232-g202: This gives the sources/materials introduced by the poetic preposition ز (here “from”). دیبای رومی is a genitive/attributive unit ‘brocade (diībā) of Rûm/Roman/Byzantine’; the second coordinated prepositional phrase uses از before تخت عاج ‘from the ivory throne’. The parallel ز ... و از ... is a rhythmic listing of origins.
segments.s232-g203: Another rhythmic list introduced by the preposition ز (“from”): تیر و کمان is the usual pair ‘arrow and bow’. The repeated ز before برگستوان keeps the same prepositional structure. The segment functions as part of a cumulative catalogue of weapons/items. (برگستوان here appears as a lexical item listed in the same slot as تیر و کمان.)
segments.s232-g204: Continues the catalog with another coordinated pair: ز گوپال (’from Gopala’) and وز خنجر هندوان, where وز is the fused poetic form of و + ز (‘and from’). خنجر هندوان is a genitive construction ‘the dagger of the Indians’. The whole unit lists another pair of sources/items; گوپال is treated as a proper name here.
segments.s232-g205: یکی introduces an indefinite/indeterminate NP ‘a certain / one’; کوه بد is a noun + adjective (noun before adjective as common in Persian poetry) meaning ‘a bad/rough mountain’. در میان دو کوه is a locative prepositional phrase ‘in the middle of two mountains’ modifying that noun phrase.
segments.s232-g206: نظاره شده is a passive/perfect participial construction: نظاره (view/observation) + شده (‘has been / was’) yielding ‘was seen/observed’. گردش is the noun ‘turning / rotation’ and اندر گروه is the prepositional phrase ‘within/among the group/troop’. Together they form the verbal or resultative clause ‘was seen turning among the troop.’
segments.s232-g207: This is a compound nominal phrase: کمان‌کش سواری pairs ‘archer’ and ‘rider/horseman’ (a compound specifying mounted archers). گشاده‌بری follows as an attributive quality ‘generosity / broad-mindedness’. The order places the descriptive quality after the noun phrase, typical of Persian poetic style.
segments.s232-g208: بتن is the prepositional-like compound بِ تن ‘on/at the body’ (here ‘on their bodies’), followed by زورمندی ‘might/strength’ as a quality borne on the body. The conjunction و links this quality to کنداوری, which here appears as a proper-name or opaque noun; the whole segment lists physical/heroic attributes worn or displayed by the riders.
segments.s232-g17: چنان شد is a two-word verbal phrase meaning 'thus/became' (چنان = 'so', شد = past 'became'). The noun phrase در و دشت آوردگاه follows and functions as the predicate: در ('gate' or 'town/gate') and دشت ('plain') joined by و, with آوردگاه ('battlefield') qualifying them — i.e. 'the gate and the plain became a battlefield.' The verb precedes the nominal predicate, a common poetic order.
segments.s232-g18: که introduces a resulting clause ('such that' / 'so that') and is followed by شد تنگ, 'became narrow.' The phrase تنگ بر مور و بر پشه راه uses بر in a poetic sense roughly 'for' or 'toward' the small creatures, so the whole clause means 'the way became narrow even for ants and gnats.' Note the adjective تنگ predicates راه (path) after the verb شد, and the subject (راه) is understood from context.
segments.s232-g19: ز بس is a causal/partitive prepositional phrase 'from/for so many' (ز = 'from', بس = 'so many/enough'), introducing the cause. کشته و خسته are coordinated past-participial/adjectival forms 'slain and weary/struck down,' and شد is the past copular 'became.' The noun phrase جوی خون ('a stream of blood') is the resulting predicate: 'a brook became/ran with blood.'
segments.s232-g20: This is an elliptical, verbless enumeration: یکی بی‌سر و دیگری سرنگون = 'one [was] headless and another [was] overthrown.' یکی = 'one', دیگری = 'the other/another'; the copula (بود/بودند) is omitted, a common poetic economy.
segments.s232-g21: چنان is an adverb 'thus/so' modifying the clause. بخت تابنده is a noun phrase where تابنده ('shining') qualifies بخت ('fortune/lot'); تاریک شد is the verb phrase 'became dark' with شد as the past copular. Word order places the modifier and noun before the predicate, typical in Persian poetic style.
segments.s232-g22: همانا is an emphatic particle 'verily/indeed.' The sequence بشب روز is a poetic juxtaposition (literally 'by-night day') meaning the coming together/approach of night and day; نزدیک شد is 'drew near' or 'approached' (نزدیک = 'approaching/near', شد = 'became/drew'). The verb ends the clause as usual.
segments.s232-g23: برآمد is the past verb 'arose/appeared.' یکی here functions as an indefinite 'a certain' or 'one' introducing the items that follow; ابر و بادی سیاه is a coordinated noun phrase 'a cloud and a black wind' (بادی used as a noun 'a wind'). The verb precedes the coordinated subject phrase for poetic effect.
segments.s232-g24: بشد (ب + شد) here functions as a past/happened sense 'it became/there was,' followed by روشنایی 'brightness/light' as the affected thing; ز خورشید و ماه means 'from the sun and moon' or 'of sun and moon.' Together: 'the light of the sun and moon was (made) [dim/obscured].' The clause places the verb before the noun phrase, a common poetic order.
segments.s232-g1: بینداخت is the past simple 'threw/cast'. آن is the demonstrative 'that'. تابداده is a compound adjective 'made to shine' (تاب + داده), post‑positive to کمند which follows as its noun: کمند 'lasso/snare'. The whole phrase = 'he cast that shining lasso.'
segments.s232-g2: سران سواران is a genitival/construct group 'the chiefs/leaders of the riders/horsemen' (سران modifies سواران). همی here is an adverbial particle giving immediacy/emphasis; کرد is the past verb 'did/made' and بند is used as 'fetter/bond' (object) meaning 'bind'. Together: 'he bound the leaders of the horsemen.'
segments.s232-g3: چو آمد is a temporal clause 'when ... came'. بنزدیک is an adverb/prepositional adverb 'near/close', and پیل سپید is a noun + postpositive adjective 'elephant (that was) white' (classical Persian often places adjectives after the noun).
segments.s232-g4: شد is the past 'became'. آن شاه چین is the subject phrase 'that king of Chin/China' (چین = the country/title here). از روان ناامید is the predicate 'hopeless in (his) spirit/soul' where از marks the domain ('with respect to/of the soul') and ناامید is the adjectival result.
segments.s232-g5: چو marks 'when'. از دستِ رستم is a possessive/agent phrase 'from Rostam's hand' (از = 'from'). رها شد is a passive‑style past 'was released/let go'. کمند appears postpositively as the topic/object 'the lasso'. The clause means 'when the lasso was released from Rostam's hand.'
segments.s232-g6: سرِ شاهِ چین is a genitive chain 'the head of the King of Chin' (سر = head). اندر آمد is 'entered/ came into' (intransitive motion into something). ببند is an imperative 'bind!' (addressing others). The sequence reports the king's head entering the snare and a command to bind him.
segments.s232-g7: ز is a poetic preposition 'from'. پیل اندر آورد is a causative/perfective sequence 'brought (him) out from the elephant' (آورد = 'brought'). و connects actions; زد بر زمین is 'struck (him) onto/upon the ground' with زد past 'struck' and بر زمین marking the target 'to the ground.'
segments.s232-g8: ببستند is a past plural 'they bound/tied'. بازوی خاقان چین is the object in construct: 'the arm of the Khaghan (supreme ruler) of Chin' (خاقان = khaghan/title, چین = the country). The clause reports that they bound the ruler's arm.
segments.s232-g97: 'مبادا که' is a negative optative clause meaning 'may it not be that / lest'. 'این کار' is the subject ('this deed/affair'), followed by the verb 'گیرد' (3rd person singular present). Here 'گیرد' means 'take' (assume) and is used after 'مبادا' to express a feared possibility; 'نشیب' is the complement meaning 'a descent/decline' — literally 'this deed takes a descent.'
segments.s232-g98: Again introduced by the negative optative 'مبادا که' ('may it not be that'), 'آید' is the verb 'come' (3rd sing.). 'بما' means 'to us' or 'upon us', while the preposition 'بر' governs the noun 'نهیب' ('rebuke, a loud reproof'); together the clause says 'may a rebuke not come upon us.'
segments.s232-g99: 'نگه کن' is an imperative ('watch! / be on the lookout!'). The particle 'که' introduces the clause explaining what to watch for ('for / that'). 'کارآگهان' (plural) literally 'those aware of affairs' functions as the subject here—i.e. 'the informants' or 'messengers'—and 'ناگهان' is an adverb 'suddenly' modifying the coming action.
segments.s232-g100: 'برند' is 3rd person plural present (from بردن 'to carry/bring') meaning 'they bring/carry'. 'آگهی' is the object 'news/information'. 'نزد' here means 'in the presence of / before', and the phrase 'شاه جهان' is a title 'king of the world' — so the clause says the informants may suddenly bring news before the king.
segments.s232-g101: 'بیاراید' is a 3rd-person present verb meaning 'he/she/it adorns/arrays.' 'آن نامور بارگاه' is the direct object: 'that renowned court/palace' (آن = that; نامور = famed, بارگاه = royal court). The verb here is narrative/descriptive present common in poetry.
segments.s232-g102: This is a compact, poetic expression meaning 'places the royal crown on (his) head.' 'نهد' (from نهاد) is the verb 'to place/set' (3rd sing.). 'کلاه' is the object 'hat/crown' qualified by 'خسروانی' = 'royal.' The sequence 'بسر بر نهد' is a poetic ordering of 'به سر برنهد / به سر نهد' ('upon the head he places'); the first item ('بسر') is an archaism/contracted form of 'به سر' and here functions as the locative 'on the head' before the verb.
segments.s232-g103: 'ببخشد' (from بخشیدن) is 3rd person singular present meaning 'he gives/bestows.' 'فراوان' is an adverb/adjective 'plentifully/many.' 'بدرویش' in the text functions as the indirect object — contextually 'to the dervish' (archaic/poetic form of 'به درویش') — and 'چیز' is the generic object 'things.' Together: 'he bestows many things upon the dervish.' (Note: the printed lemma senses for 'بدرویش' do not match this syntactic role, so index -1 is used.)
segments.s232-g104: 'که' introduces a subordinate clause explaining the wished-for result: 'that upon his life may be praise.' 'بر جان او' = 'upon his life/soul' (locative), 'آفرین' here is a formulaic noun 'praise/acclamation,' and 'باد' is the optative particle 'may it be.' 'نیز' adds 'also/too.' The whole clause is a conventional blessing: 'may praise be upon his life as well.'
segments.s232-g241: This short clause is a poetic, somewhat archaic locution: وز ایدر functions like از ایدر (“from here”) with وز a condensed/poetic form of a preposition; ایدر = “here.” شوم is used adjectivally as “ill‑omened/ill‑fated” (not the 1st‑person verb), followed by the plural noun تازیان “the Arabs.” چون + پلنگ is a simile “like a leopard.” Word order is topical (location first), then modifier + head (شوم تازیان), then the simile.
segments.s232-g242: درنگی نه is a negated verb/verb‑noun construction meaning “did not pause/did not hesitate” (نه after the verbal noun is a negative particle in this poetic register). والا بود is an adjectival predicate “was exalted/noble” (والا before بود), and مرد سنگ is a noun phrase 'man (مرد) of stone (سنگ)' — a compressed epithet meaning steadfast or like a rock. The clause thus links the Arabs’ swift action to the steadfast, noble man.
segments.s232-g243: کسی کو here functions as an older relative construction meaning “anyone who / he who” (کو = classical relative ‘who/that’). گنهکار و خونی بود is a predicate: two adjectival descriptors ‘guilty/sinful’ and ‘blood‑stained,’ followed by بود (past copula) — ‘was guilty and bloody.’
segments.s232-g244: بکشور is a locative phrase ‘in/to the country/realm.’ بمانی is the present/subjunctive form of ماندن ‘remain’ used with the past copula بود to express a past customary/result (‘would remain’). زبونی بود is the resultative predicate ‘there would be abasement/humiliation.’ The sequence places the location first, then the remaining/descriptive result.
segments.s232-g245: زمین را is the object with the accusative marker را (‘the earth’). بخنجر is an instrumental phrase ‘with a dagger.’ بشویم is an archaic/poetic verbal form (subjunctive/optative) here expressing intent/resolve — effectively ‘I will wash’ (i.e. cleanse by violence); ز + کین = ‘out of/for vengeance.’ The clause therefore expresses a vowed action: ‘I will wash the land with a dagger because of vengeance.’
segments.s232-g246: بدان را is a demonstrative object ‘that one’ (بدان = ‘to/that’) with را marking it as definite object. نمانم is the negative first‑person form of ماندن ‘I will not remain / I will not leave’ (here used transitively in the sense ‘I will not leave him’). همی is an emphatic/continuous particle (poetic), and بر زمین = ‘on the ground.’ Together: ‘I will not leave that one on the ground.’
segments.s232-g247: بدو گفت گودرز is inverted word order for reported speech: بدو ‘to him,’ گفت (here used as the speech verb ‘said’), and گودرز the speaker’s name — literally ‘to him Goudarz said.’ کای is the vocative particle ‘O,’ and نیک رای is a compound epithet ‘of good counsel’ (نیک = good, رای = advice/counsel).
segments.s232-g248: تو is the addressed ‘you.’ تا here has a purposive/so‑that sense (‘so that/in order that’). جای ماند is a verbless clause meaning ‘the place remain(s)’ (جای = place; ماند = ‘may remain’ / third‑person), followed by بمانی, second‑person subjunctive/imperative ‘you remain.’ بجای (‘in place/in your post’) closes the command. The structure uses a three‑part purposive formula: ‘You — so that your place remain — remain in place.’
segments.s232-g25: This is an idiomatic, compact past clause: سر از پای ... ندانست literally 'did not know head from foot.' ندانست is past tense 3rd-person singular of دانستن. The phrase سر از پای (head from foot) expresses utter confusion or inability to distinguish parts of the enemy; باز ('again') is placed here as an adverbial linking to the following action.
segments.s232-g26: گرفتند is past tense 3rd-person plural 'they took/ seized' and combines with بیابان (taking to the waste/desert is an idiom for fleeing). The coordination و links this verb phrase to the following noun phrase راه دراز ('the long road'), so the whole segment means 'they took to the desert and (took) the long road.'
segments.s232-g27: نگه کرد is a past-tense compound meaning 'kept watch' or 'looked on' (کرد as a light verb). The verb appears before the subject پیران (a not-uncommon poetic word order), so 'نگه کرد پیران' = 'the elders watched.' The sequence ب + دان here forms an archaic/contracted pointing ('to/at that') attaching to کارزار ('that battle') — in other words 'they watched that battle.'
segments.s232-g28: This is a verbless, nominal phrase describing what the elders saw: برگشتن is the infinitive used as a noun 'the turning' and روزگار is its logical possessor/subject 'of time/fate.' چنان تیز is an adverbial modifier ('so/so very swift/keen'), so the phrase reads 'such a swift turning of fate' (i.e., how swiftly fortune changed).
segments.s232-g29: The segment begins a negative enumeration with نه ... listing absent/high-ranking names: نه introduces the first set of items. The nouns are simply juxtaposed and connected by و; here منشور, فرطوس, and خاقان چین are items in that list. This is a poetic catalog without an explicit verb (the negation applies to the whole list).
segments.s232-g30: The repeated نه continues the negation for a second group: آن (that/those) introduces 'those famed ones' (نامداران) and مردان کین is a compound meaning 'men of vengeance/hatred' with کین postpositive as an attributive noun. Again, no finite verb is present; the clause completes the 'neither... nor...' structure.
segments.s232-g31: This is a simple past clause with the direct object fronted: درفش بزرگان نگونسار is 'the woeful banner of the great ones' (بزرگان in genitive). نگونسار is a postpositive adjective 'mournful/ill‑fated.' The verb دید (3rd-person past) closes the clause: 'he saw the mournful banner of the elders.'
segments.s232-g32: This clause repeats the past verb دید and gives the object and locative: بخاک اندرون ('to the ground within' / 'inside on the earth') gives location/condition, followed by خستگان خوار ('the weary, despised') where خوار is a postpositive adjective 'humbled/despised.' Grammatically it means 'he saw the weary within humbled to the dust.'
segments.s232-g225: به + noun here marks the circumstance or object of the emotion (roughly “with/regarding”). The noun phrase گنج و بانبوه is coordinated by و, and بودند is the past‑pl. copula “they were” placed before the predicate adjective شاد, a typical Persian order (compound nominal predicate). بانبوه is an adverbial/qualifying adjective meaning “in abundance/abundant.”
segments.s232-g226: زمانی introduces a temporal clause “for a time/for a while.” ز + یزدان is the poetic genitive/agent marker “of/from God.” نکردند is the past negative of کردن (they did not do) functioning as the verb in the clause, and یاد is the noun “remembrance,” so یاد نکردند = “they did not remember.”
segments.s232-g227: که is a relative pronoun “who/that” referring back to the (implied) divine subject. The coordinated phrase چرخ سپهر و زمان lists cosmic entities (چرخ here as the cosmic wheel/sky) and the verb آفرید (past tense) follows, standard classical Persian V‑final order: (that) who the heaven‑wheel and time created.
segments.s232-g228: بسی here is an intensifier meaning “many/much” (classical usage); آشکار و نهان are coordinated opposites “manifest and hidden.” The verb آفرید repeats to predicate that many things both open and concealed were created. Word order again places the verb at the end.
segments.s232-g229: ز marks source or origin (“from”). یزدان‌شناس is a compound meaning a person who knows or reveres God (a pious/theologian figure), coordinated with بیزدان “the godless.” سپاس is the noun “praise/thanks”; the short phrase indicates that praise comes from both kinds of people.
segments.s232-g230: بدو is the cliticial prepositional fusion meaning “to him/unto Him.” بگرود is an older/formal verb form meaning “turns to / is directed to” (here indicating the movement of praise toward God); مرد نیکی‌شناس is the subject phrase “the man who knows goodness” (a grateful/virtuous person) that carries out or is associated with this action. Word order places the indirect object before the verb and the actor after it, a poetic inversion.
segments.s232-g231: کزو is a compound of ک + ز + او meaning “from which/from whom.” بودمان is an older/poetic past form meaning roughly “there was to us / we had” (i.e. ‘belonged to us’), followed by a coordinated list زور و فر و هنر (“strength, glory (fravashi/royal fortune), and skill”). The clause states these blessings derive from that source.
segments.s232-g232: ازو again marks origin “from him.” دردمندی here denotes suffering/affliction (or being afflicted); هم adds “also/too.” The following ز (poetic from/of) + و (and) link the final item گهر “jewel/gem,” so the clause contrasts that the same source gave both hardship and treasure. The verbless style is classical: origin + items, with the verb understood from the previous clause.
segments.s232-g209: خدنگی بینداختی چارپر is verb-final poetic word order with the object and target placed before the verb. خدنگی (خدنگ + ـی) is the direct object 'a dagger' (ـی here is the indefinite/direct-object-like ending in epic diction), چارپر is a proper name (the target), and بینداختی is the 2nd-person singular past/preterite 'you threw/cast'. The sequence reads literally 'a dagger you threw at Chārpar.'
segments.s232-g210: This clause expresses inability or failure of movement: ازین سو (از +ین+ سو) = 'from this side' and بدان سو (ب + دان + سو) = 'to that side' (a compacted poetic form for به آن سو). نکردی گذر is a composed verbal phrase where نکردی ('you did not do') combines with گذر ('pass, passage') to mean 'you did not make a passage / you did not pass.' Overall: 'you did not pass from this side to that side.'
segments.s232-g211: چو introduces a temporal clause here meaning 'when.' رستم is the subject. نگه کرد is a light-verb construction where نگه (gaze/look) is the noun and کرد 'did/made' is the past tense verb: 'he cast a look.' خیره بماند is a verbless/result phrase (خیره = 'astonished/staring') with the present-subjunctive/optative-looking form بماند used narratively to mean 'he remained / stood (in that state)'.
segments.s232-g212: جهان‌آفرین را is the direct object phrase 'to/the Creator' marked by را. فراوان is an adverbial modifier 'repeatedly/abundantly.' بخواند is a past-tense (narrative) 3rd-person singular form of خواندن used here in the sense 'he recited / he invoked / he called upon' — in context a prayerful or calling action directed at the Creator.
segments.s232-g213: چنین گفت is a short reporting formula 'thus he said.' Here گفت functions as the narrative 'said' (the provided sense list is conversational; in epic diction it is the speech-verb). کین روز in older/poetic Persian works as a demonstrative 'this day' (kین = 'this' in some archaic uses) or can carry an undertone of hostility; ناپایدار is the predicate adjective 'unstable/transient.' The clause reads 'Thus he said: this day is unstable.'
segments.s232-g214: The pair گهی ... گهی introduces alternation 'sometimes ... sometimes ...' بزم سازد is a verb phrase where بزم (feast/banquet) is the thing produced and سازد (3rd sg. present/narrative of ساختن) means 'it causes/creates/produces' — 'it brings feasting.' کارزار is the alternate object 'battle/struggle.' The line therefore contrasts feast and battle as alternating states of the day.
segments.s232-g215: همی گردَد is a habitual/continuous narrative formula: همی (an older/adverbial particle, 'continually/indeed') + گردد (3rd-sg. present/subjunctive of گشتن used narratively = 'it occurs/comes about'). این خواسته = 'this desire/this thing wished for/this affair' (خواسته as noun). زان برین is a compacted archaic/poetic locution meaning roughly 'from that (above/from that source)' — زان = 'from that', and برین here is an elevated adverbial form ('to/from that higher place' in epic diction).
segments.s232-g216: بنفرین is an exclamatory/formulaic noun/verb form meaning 'a curse' or the imperative 'curse!' used nominally; بود is the past-tense copula 'was' (here stating that a curse existed/fell). گه گهی biconsists of duplicative adverbial particles meaning 'at times / from time to time.' بفرین is again the imperative/exclamatory 'curse!' or the noun 'a curse' used like an interjection. The clause narrates that a curse followed, appearing occasionally and even as an outcry.
segments.s232-g57: چنین is an adverb meaning “thus/so” placed before the reporting verb. گفت is the narrative past “said”; narrative style often places the verb before the subject, so گفت رستم = “Rostam said.” بایرانیان is an older/poetic form corresponding to به ایرانیان (to the Iranians) and functions as the indirect object.
segments.s232-g58: که introduces a subordinate clause (“that”). اکنون is the time adverb “now.” بباید is a literary/present-modal form meaning “it is necessary / one must” used with an infinitive. گشادن is the infinitive “to open,” and میان is a relational word “between / midst,” completing the verb’s object idea.
segments.s232-g59: بپیش is a prepositional/adverbial phrase meaning “before, in the presence of.” It governs the following nominal phrase جهاندار پیروزگر, where جهاندار “sovereign/holder of the world” is followed by the epithet پیروزگر “victorious/triumphant.” The adjective comes after the noun, a common word order in classical Persian.
segments.s232-g60: نه ... نه is the correlative negative pair “neither ... nor.” باید here is an impersonal/modal “is fitting/ought to be” (present). گوپال is a lowly figure (“cowherd”) used generically; بند کمر = “belt / girdle of the waist” (بند as strap/bond + کمر “waist”), a nominal phrase negated by the correlative.
segments.s232-g61: همه is the universal subject “everyone.” سر بخاک سیه بر نهید is a verbal phrase: بخاک سیه “to the black earth” + بر نهید (نهاد in its poetic/imperative/present form) “place/put on.” The verb نهید is an older literary form of نهادن (“to place”), so the clause functions as an injunction: “all place their heads on the black earth.”
segments.s232-g62: کزین پس is a fixed classical sequence meaning “from this time forth / henceforth.” همه تاج بر سر نهید repeats the verb نهید with تاج “crown” as object and بر سر “on the head” as the locative phrase; again نهید = place/put, so the sense is “henceforth let all set a crown on their head.”
segments.s232-g63: کزین = “from/among these.” نامدارن is the plural “the famed/renowned ones.” یکی نیست کم is a compact classical negation: یکی نیست = “there is not one,” followed by کم “insufficient/less” — together meaning “there is not a single inferior one among them” or “none of them is lacking/insufficient.”
segments.s232-g64: که introduces a clause of result/explanation (“for/that”). اکنون = “now.” شدستی is an archaic/literary verbal form here representing a perfect/resultadof شدن (“has become”)—this form is poetical and not the modern standard; its exact morphological labeling is old-fashioned. دل ما دژم = “our heart(s) [is] sullen/grim,” with دژم an adjective meaning “sullen.”
segments.s232-g169: چو here introduces a hypothetical/conditional clause ('if' / 'should you say'). گویی is the verb-form meaning 'you say' or 'one might say' (poetic present), followed by که introducing the subordinate clause. روزی is an adverb 'one day / someday', تن‌آسان is a plural/collective noun phrase 'the easy-bodied / those who live in ease', and شوند is the 3rd-person plural subjunctive/potential of شدن, giving 'will become' or 'may become' and agreeing with تن‌آسان.
segments.s232-g170: ز is the preposition 'because of / from' introducing the cause or motive. تیمار ایران is the noun phrase 'the care/guarding of Iran' (تیمار + modifier ایران). هراسان شوند is the verb phrase 'become frightened'—هراسان (adjective/participial) + شوند (3rd pl. subjunctive/potential of شدن), and it agrees with the subject implied from the previous segment.
segments.s232-g171: ازین پس is a fixed temporal phrase 'from now on / henceforth' (از + این + پس). تو is the addressee 'you'. پیران و کلباد را is a coordinated noun phrase 'the elders and Kalbad' followed by the object marker را; the object marker flags these people as the entities being acted on (the verb is implied in the following segment).
segments.s232-g172: چو here means 'as / like' introducing a comparison. هومان و رویین و پولاد are three names/epithets in a coordinated list; را at the end again marks the whole list as the object/topic treated in the same way. The clause is elliptical (the verb is omitted) and supplies the manner in which the people in the previous segment should be dealt with—i.e. 'treat X as you treat Y.'
segments.s232-g173: نگه کن is an imperative construction: نگه (root meaning 'look/watch') + کن (imperative auxiliary 'do') = 'keep watch' or 'look!'. بدین is an older/poetic form meaning 'to this / over this' (بـ + دین/این) and دشت is the direct object 'plain'. با لشکری is an instrumental phrase 'with an army' (با + لشکری), specifying how the watch is to be kept.
segments.s232-g174: تو در کشوری places 'you' in a location ('you in a country/region') where تو is the subject and در + کشوری gives the locative. رستم از کشوری is a contrasting/location phrase 'Rostam in (another) country' where از signals separation/contrast ('from/other than')—the clause is terse/elliptical and states the intended deployment: you here, Rostam elsewhere.
segments.s232-g175: اگر is the conditional 'if'. تاو is an older/poetic noun meaning roughly 'strength / courage / might' (not matched by the supplied sense list) and دارید is the 2nd-person plural/polite present 'you have'. جنگ آورید is a jussive/imperative-like plural form ('make war' / 'wage battle') where جنگ (war) combines with آورید (subjunctive/imperative of آوردن) to mean 'wage war'.
segments.s232-g176: مرا (here split in the list as م + را) is the accusative 'me' (را is the object marker). زین can mean 'from this / from the saddle' in poetic usage (here 'from this' or 'from my saddle'), and سپس means 'afterwards / after this'. کی is an interrogative 'who / when' (here 'who'). بچنگ آورید is a compound verb 'bring into the grasp / seize' (ب + چنگ آوردن), with آورید again in the subjunctive/jussive form; the whole asks rhetorically who after this would be able to seize/take me captive.
segments.s232-g257: This is a nominal descriptive phrase listing three adjectives/epithets: هنرمند (skillful), با دانش (with learning = learned), بانژاد (of noble birth). Word order is adjective/phrase sequence modifying an implied subject; با here is a preposition meaning 'with' used attributively (با دانش = 'learned'). بانژاد is an older compound meaning 'by/with noble lineage' and functions like an adjective.
segments.s232-g258: تو شادان is a verbless predication common in Persian: subject pronoun تو followed by predicate adjective شادان (‘joyful’). The conjunction و links this clause to the next: کاوس شاه از تو شاد literally 'Kavus the king [is] glad from/of you' — here از marks cause ('because of you'). The final شاد is the predicate adjective for کاوس; the copula is omitted.
segments.s232-g259: یکی رنج برگیر is an imperative construction: یکی ('someone' / 'one of you') + رنج ('hardship/task') + برگیر (imperative 'take up'). The following ز ایدر برو uses poetic ز (= از, 'from') + ایدر (archaic 'here') and برو (imperative 'go'), so the sequence is an instruction to someone to take the task and depart from here.
segments.s232-g260: ببر is an imperative 'take/carry' (not the animal); نامه من is the direct object ('my letter' — من here functions as the genitive/possessive), and بر before شاه means 'toward/to' (direction/recipient). شاه نو = 'the new king' (noun + adjective). The whole is a command: carry my letter to the new king.
segments.s232-g261: ابا is an older verb meaning 'to be unwilling/reluctant' and here functions imperatively/elliptically as 'do not be reluctant' (an exhortation). خویشتن ('self') is emphatic/reflexive ('yourself' / 'with you'); بستگان را is the definite direct object ('the relatives') marked by را; ببر is the imperative 'take'. The sequence means 'do not be reluctant; take your relatives'.
segments.s232-g262: This continues the command with two coordinated objects: هیونان ('beasts/animals') and این خواسته ('this wish/request'). سربسر is a postposed adverb meaning 'entirely' or 'wholly' (here modifying خواسته). The implied verb bبر from the previous segment governs these nouns: 'take the beasts and this entire request as well.'
segments.s232-g263: همان at the start means 'the same' or 'that very', applying to the following list: افسر (here 'helmet' rather than 'officer'), یاره ('attendant/follower'), گرز ('mace/club'), and تاج ('crown'). The items are coordinated by و in normal Persian noun-list order.
segments.s232-g264: Again همان 'the same' prefaces a second group: ژنده پیلان is a compound where ژنده ('rag/cloth') qualifies پیلان (pl. of پیل, 'elephant') — here meaning elephant trappings/cloths. و هم uses هم as 'also' before تخت عاج ('ivory throne'). Word order keeps the modifier (ژنده) before the noun (پیلان) and هم before تخت to add 'also'.
segments.s232-g217: زمانه is the subject ('the age / the times'). نماند is the verb with negative prefix ن- plus ماند (to remain) giving 'does not remain/last'. بآرام خویش is an adverbial phrase: ب- 'in/at' + آرام 'calm' and خویش + enclitic ش 'its' = 'in its calm'. Word order is Subject + Verb + adverbial; the enclitic ش attaches to خوی to mark possession.
segments.s232-g218: چنینست is an impersonal copular phrase 'it is thus / such is (the case)'. تا introduces a temporal/conditional clause meaning 'as long as / until'. بود is the literary copula 'is/exists' (here existential rather than simple past). آیین و کیش form a coordinated noun phrase 'ritual and creed'. The clause thus reads: 'Such is (it) as long as ritual and creed exist.'
segments.s232-g219: یکی quantifies 'one / a certain (one)'. گنج is the direct object 'treasure'. ازین سان is a compound adverb 'از + این + سان' = 'in this way / thus' (here written split). همی is the classical progressive/continuous marker (before the verb) meaning 'continually / is continually'. پرورد is the verb 'nurtures/cares for' (archaic 3rd-singular form). Order: Quantifier + Object + manner phrase + progressive marker + verb.
segments.s232-g220: یکی دیگر 'another one' (یکی + دیگر). آید is the verb 'comes'. کزو is a fused relative/adverbial 'from which / from whom' (introducing source or cause). برخورد here is a noun (or verbal noun) 'collision / encounter' placed after the relative, so the clause reads 'another comes from which (there is) a clash/encounter.'
segments.s232-g221: بران is an adverb 'thereupon / on that account' placed before the copula. بود is the copular verb 'was / there were'. کاموس here is a proper name (not the dictionary sense) and forms the first coordinate of the subject. و links it to خاقان چین 'the Khaghan of China' (title + place). Word order is adverb + copula + coordinated subject.
segments.s232-g222: که here is the relative pronoun 'who / which'. آتش is the object 'fire'. برآرد is a literary/poetic 3rd-singular form 'brings forth / sends'. ز is the preposition 'from' (poetic form), and ایران‌زمین is the object of that preposition 'the land of Iran'. The relative clause modifies the preceding rulers: 'who would bring fire from Iran-land.'
segments.s232-g223: بدین is the prepositional phrase 'به + این' meaning 'with / by this' (written here split into ب + دین); it introduces the means/instrument. ژنده modifies پیلان (ژنده پیلان = 'tattered / ragged elephants') with پیلان the plural of پیل 'elephant'. و این خواسته adds 'and this خواسته' — خواسته here means 'demand / the requested thing'. The phrase lists the things by which or with which something was done.
segments.s232-g224: بدین again 'with this' (ب + دین). لشکر و گنج is the coordinated noun phrase 'the army and the treasure' (objects/recipients of the previous means). آراسته is a past-participle/adjectival verb 'adorned / arrayed' used predicatively; the construction means 'by these things the army and treasure were thus made ready/arrayed.'
segments.s232-g41: چو is the subordinating temporal 'when'. او را is the object 'him' plus the enclitic object marker را. ندیدند is past-tense plural with the negative prefix ن- (they did not see). گشتند is the past plural of گشتن used here as 'they turned/returned', and باز is an adverb 'back/again' modifying گشتند. The whole clause is a temporal subordinate: 'When they did not see him, (they) turned back.'
segments.s232-g42: دلیران is the plural nominative 'the brave ones' (subject/agent, though the verb appears in the previous segment). سوی is the directional preposition 'toward'. رستم is the proper name, and سرفراز is an epithet placed after the name meaning 'victorious/proud'. The noun phrase attaches to the verb of the previous segment: 'the brave (turned) toward Rostam the proud.'
segments.s232-g43: تبه گشته is a perfect-participial adjectival phrase meaning 'having become ruined/bedraggled' (تبه here 'ruin/mishap' used adjectivally + گشته 'become/been'). اسپان جنگی is the noun 'war-horses' (اسپان = horses, جنگی qualifying them as 'of war'). ز کار is a causal/ablative phrase 'from the work/toil' indicating the reason. Word order is adjectival phrase + noun + causal phrase.
segments.s232-g44: همه is the quantifier 'all/everyone' applying to the referred group. رنجه and خسته are predicative/adjectival states 'distressed/suffering' and 'tired/weary', joined by و. کارزار 'battle/field' gives the context/cause (they are suffering and weary from the battle). The short verbless clause is typical: adjectives follow the quantifier and are linked to the prior noun phrase.
segments.s232-g45: برفتند is the past plural 'they went away/they departed' (بر- + رفتند). با کام دل is an instrumental/attitudinal phrase 'with (the) heart's desire' (کام = desire, دل = heart; together an idiom meaning with satisfaction or eager will). سوی کوه is the directional phrase 'toward the mountain'. These adverbial phrases modify the verb برفتند.
segments.s232-g46: تهمتن is the proper name/epithet (Tahmatan/the mighty one). بپیش is an adverbial/positional 'forward/ahead' (literally 'to the front'). اندرون functions as a locative/adverbial 'into the interior/inside'. با گروه is an accompaniment phrase 'with the company/troop'. The verb is omitted here (understood from the previous clause), so this sequence describes Tahmatan going ahead into the inner place with his band.
segments.s232-g47: همه is the universal quantifier 'all'. ترگ و جوشن is a coordinated noun phrase 'rags and cuirass(es)' (ترگ = rags/shreds, جوشن = armor). به خون و به خاک uses the preposition به repeated for emphasis: 'in/with blood and in/with dust/earth' — describing their state of being covered/soiled. The phrase describes appearance: everything (rags and armor) is soaked/covered with blood and earth.
segments.s232-g48: شده is the perfect-participial 'have become' and governs the following predicates. غرق here is adjectival/participial 'drenched/immersed' (i.e. soaked in blood). و links the next phrase. برگستوان in classical usage here is a noun phrase (contextually something like part of the arms/garment) and چاک چاک is a reduplicated adjective 'split/torn, torn to pieces' (چاک repeated for emphasis). Together the clause says they 'had become drenched and (their ...) rent and torn.' The verb is the same شده applied to both predicates.
segments.s232-g193: This phrase is a prepositional + noun phrase followed by a modal: ازین هدیه (az-in hedye) = “from these gifts” (از + این, here spelled ازین). شاه باید places the subject (شاه) before the modal بایـد (“must/ought”); نخست is an adverb “first” that scopes over what follows. Word order is topical (prepositional phrase → subject → modal → adverb) and the clause is elliptical (the main verb that finishes the obligation is supplied in the next segment).
segments.s232-g194: پس آنگه = “then / after that” (two adverbs stacked for emphasis). مرا و ترا appear here split into clitics in the gloss: م + را (m + accusative marker) and ت + را (t + accusative marker) — together meaning مرا و ترا (“me and you”) and marked as definite/direct objects by را. بهر is the preposition “for / on account of,” and جست is the past of جستن (“to seek / look for”). The verb جست takes the object مرا و ترا (marked by را) and the prepositional phrase بهر to express purpose/aim.
segments.s232-g195: بدان دشت = a locative phrase: (ب + دان) here functions as ‘in/at that’ (poetic/archaic form) + دشت “plain.” بسیار شاهان بدند is an archaic past plural copula: بدند corresponds to classical بودَند/بودند “they were.” The order places the place first (بدان دشت), then the quantified subject (بسیار شاهان) and the copular verb at the end (بدند), a typical Old/poetic Persian word-order.
segments.s232-g196: همه نامداران گیهان بدند: همه (all) quantifies the following noun phrase نامداران (the renowned/illustrious ones). گیهان is a poetic variant of جهان ‘the world’; بدند again is the past plural ‘they were.’ The clause therefore means that all the world’s famed persons were (present) — adjective/noun phrases precede the verb in this poetic register.
segments.s232-g197: This is an enumerative prepositional series: ز چین و ز سقلاب وز هند و وهر — the particle ز (ze) = “from” is repeated in the poetic list (وز here is orthographically ו + ز = ‘and from’). The items are place names (چین = China; سقلاب/Saqlāb = a foreign/place-name; هند = India; وهر = an archaic/obscure place term). The repeated ز is a classical poetic device to link origins.
segments.s232-g198: همه گنج داران گیرنده شهر is a noun phrase with quantifier (همه = all) + compound noun گنج‌داران (treasure‑holders/treasurers). گیرنده is a participial/agentive form (literally “taker/one who takes”), here functioning as an adjective describing those who had taken the city; شهر is the (direct) object/noun 'the city'. In poetry participles often stand where a relative clause would in prose: “all the treasure-holders who took the city.”
segments.s232-g199: سپهبد بیامد is a simple past clause: سپهبد (spahbed, the commander) + بیامد (came). همه گرد کرد uses گرد (around/about) plus the causative/performative کرد (“made/did”) to mean “he gathered everyone” — literally “he made (them) turn about/all together.” This is a common poetic way to express assembling people.
segments.s232-g200: برفتند is past plural “they went/they departed.” گردان here denotes the military unit “troop(s).” بدشت = (ب + دشت) “to/toward the plain” (instrumental/locative formation), and نبرد is the noun “battle.” Together the phrase states that the troops departed to the plain for the battle; the verb appears early (برفتند) and the purpose/place follow, as is normal in Persian narrative.
segments.s232-g233: سپه بود = 'there was an army': سپه (noun) is the subject (sense: army/host) and بود is the past copula 'was'. و هم connects the next clause ('and also'), and گنج آباد بود is a nominal clause where گنج ('treasury' / treasure‑house) is described by the adjective آباد ('prosperous/filled'); the second بود is again the past copula. Word order is Subject — Copula; then conjunction — SubjectPhrase — Copula.
segments.s232-g234: سگالش is an obscure/rare noun here (its exact meaning is uncertain), serving as the topic/agent of the following predicate. همه کار = 'in all affairs' (همه modifies کار = 'all/every affair'). بیداد بود is a predicate: بیداد 'injustice/oppression' + بود 'was'. The clause therefore says that (whatever) سگالش was, in all matters there was oppression.
segments.s232-g235: کنون is a temporal adverb 'now/at present'. از بزرگان هر کشوری is a prepositional phrase: از 'from' + بزرگان 'the great ones' (i.e. leading men), with هر کشوری ('of every country') postposed to specify scope — a typical poetic word order where the quantifier/possessive phrase follows the noun it qualifies.
segments.s232-g236: گزیده = 'selected/chosen' functions as a noun or substantive adjective ('the chosen one[s]'). ز is the poetic form of از ('from'). هر کشوری repeats the distributive idea 'from each land', and مهتری is the noun 'chief/elder' (here appositive/predicate: 'a chosen chief from each country'). Word order (گزیده ... مهتری) places the participial adjective before the substantive it specifies.
segments.s232-g237: بدین here functions as 'with/by this' (poetic) but in the provided tokenization it is split into ب + دین; neither of those lemmas fits this use exactly, so they are marked as irregular. ژنده پیلان = 'ragged/threadbare elephants' — ژنده (ragged) modifies پیلان (archaic/plural form of پیل 'elephant'). فرستم is first‑person 'I send' (present/historic present used to narrate dispatch), and بشاه is the dative 'to the king' ('بشاه' = 'to the king').
segments.s232-g238: همان is a demonstrative 'that very/the same'. تخت زرین = 'golden throne' (noun + adjective). و links the coordinated noun phrase to زرین کلاه = 'golden cap/hat' (here 'crown'). Adjective زرین 'golden' follows the noun in this construction for poetic emphasis.
segments.s232-g239: همان خواسته = 'that same desired thing/request' (همان emphasises identity; خواسته is a noun 'wish/what is desired'). بر = 'upon/to' here marks the target, هیونان (plural of هیون) = 'beasts/monsters', and مست 'drunken/intoxicated' is a postpositive adjective modifying هیونان (figurative for frenzied/ferocious). The whole phrase indicates the thing is being directed to those beasts.
segments.s232-g240: فرستم again is 1st person 'I send' (narrative present). سزاوار is a predicative adjective 'deserving/fit for'. چیزی که هست is a relative phrase 'something that is' (چیزی = 'something'; که introduces the relative clause; هست = copula 'is'); together سزاوار چیزی که هست means 'worthy of what it is' — idiomatically here 'they are sent what they deserve'.
segments.s232-g129: می‌خسروانی is a poetic compound: می (wine) + خسروانی (kingly/royal), functioning as a modifier meaning “royal wine.” بیاورد is a third‑person singular past/narrative verb here (‘brought’); word order is verb phrase before the coordinated noun جام. و simply coordinates the two objects (royal wine and the cup).
segments.s232-g130: This is a short nominal clause where نام (here meaning ‘name’/‘fame’) is governed by the verb برد used in a sense of obtaining/taking (poetic past). نِخستین is an adjective ‘the first’ understood to modify جام from the previous segment; ز functions as the poetic preposition ‘from’/‘derived from’ and links شاه جهان (‘King of the World’) as the source. Grammatically برد acts as a past verb ‘took/obtained’ so the phrase means ‘the first (cup) took its name from the King of the World.’
segments.s232-g131: بزد is a past tense verb ‘he struck/blew’ (narrative past). کرنای is the direct object ‘the trumpet’ (with ezāfe omitted in poetry); از بر here gives a local sense ‘from/at the side of’ and precedes the noun phrase ژنده پیل ‘the tattered/ragged elephant’ (adjective before noun is a common poetic order).
segments.s232-g132: همی رفت is a fixed poetic/periphrastic construction meaning ‘kept going’ or ‘was borne onward’ (همی gives durative/continuous aspect; رفت is the past of رفتن used narratively). آوازشان ‘their voice/sound’ is the subject of رفت (possessive suffix -شان). بر here expresses extent/over (’over/for’) and دو میل is the measure of distance; the clause means ‘their sound continued/resonated over two miles.’
segments.s232-g133: چو introduces a temporal clause ‘when.’ خرم شد is a copular/Resultative sequence ‘became joyful/gleeful.’ از here gives the cause or locus (‘at/from’) and links to می‌رخ پهلوان, a poetic expression for the champion’s countenance/beauty (می‌رخ = face/beautiful look, پهلوان = champion). The clause thus reads ‘when the champion’s countenance became joyful.’
segments.s232-g134: برفتند is past plural ‘they went/off they departed’ (narrative past). The following adjectives شادان و روشن‌روان qualify the subject (they) as manner complements: ‘joyful and bright‑souled/cheerful.’ The descriptive words follow the verb for poetic emphasis.
segments.s232-g135: چو introduces a temporal/simile clause ‘when/as.’ پیراهن شب is a metaphorical object ‘the garment/covering of night’ (پیراهن = covering/robe), and بدرید is a past transitive verb ‘tore.’ ماه is the agent/subject ‘the moon.’ The line is a poetic image: ‘when the moon tore the night’s garment.’
segments.s232-g136: نهاد here is the past of نهادن ‘placed/put’ (poetic past). از بر combines to indicate position ‘upon/on’ (از بر + noun is a literary locative). چرخ means ‘sky/heaven’ and پیروزه‌گاه is a descriptive noun for the vault/place of the sky, literally ‘turquoise‑adorned place’ or ‘place of victory’ in different readings; together the phrase says the moon set upon the turquoise‑studded heaven. Grammatically, the verb (نهاد) takes the locative phrase that follows as its complement.
segments.s232-g185: ازو (a literary form of از + او) means 'from him/it' and precedes the object. چیز is the object 'a thing, something'. بستان here is an imperative verb 'take!' (classical Persian; the provided lexical senses did not list the verbal sense), followed by و 'and'. پایش is پای + the enclitic -ش 'his', literally 'his foot(s)'; ببند is an imperative 'bind/fasten' (ببند imperative of بستن). Word order is imperative Verb + (object) then imperative Verb for the second command; the enclitic -ش attaches to پای to mark possessor.
segments.s232-g186: نگه کن is a compound imperative made of نگه (a noun/Verbal-noun meaning 'watch/keeping') + کن 'do!' = 'keep watch' or 'station (someone) to watch'. یکی 'one' introduces an agent or person to be stationed. پشت پیلی بلند literally 'on the back of a tall elephant' (پشت = back, پیلی = 'elephantine' / elephant, بلند = 'high/tall'). The phrase assigns where that one should be placed.
segments.s232-g187: بدین سان is an idiomatic phrase meaning 'in this manner/thus' (a literary compound). فرستش is فرست (send) + enclitic -ش 'him/it', functioning as 'send him'; بنزدیک is the prepositional phrase ب + نزدیک 'to near/close to'; شاه = 'the king'. Word order: manner phrase first, then the verb+clitic and the destination.
segments.s232-g188: مگر here means 'unless'. پخته is a predicative adjective/participle 'cooked/ripe/prepared' used metaphorically 'made ready'. گردد is the optative/subjunctive/passive-form of شدن 'become' (here 'be made/become'); بدان is ب + آن 'to that' (i.e. 'to that'); بارگاه = 'royal court/palace'. The clause as a whole is a conditional rider: 'unless he is first made ready for that court.'
segments.s232-g189: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. یاقوت 'ruby' and گوهر 'gem' are coordinated items; وز here is the archaic fused sequence و + ز meaning 'and from' (a poetic contraction), not the literal senses listed for وز. و is the ordinary 'and' joining تخت 'throne' and عاج 'ivory' (عاج modifies تخت: 'an ivory throne'). The line lists valuable items, using compact poetic coordination.
segments.s232-g190: ز دینار begins another 'from ...' phrase ('from dinars' = money). وز again represents the fused poetic 'and from'. افسر here means 'helmet' (classical usage), followed by و 'and' plus گنج 'treasure' and و تاج 'and crown'. The whole segment continues the catalogue of gifts.
segments.s232-g191: نگر is an imperative 'look/consider'. تا که here introduces the indirect question 'so that/that' — 'look to see what...' (تا + که = 'to see what'). دارد is the present-tense 'has/possesses'. ز ایران سپاه = 'an army from Iran' (ز = 'from', ایران = the land/people of Iran, سپاه = 'army').
segments.s232-g192: همه یکسره is an adverbial cluster 'all, altogether/completely'. خواسته is a participle 'wishing/desired' used here with خواه to express that they wish/ask; پیش means 'in the presence of / before (the king)'. خواه is the verb 'want/ask' (presentive), so the phrase says that they all together desire/ask to appear before (the king).
segments.s232-g137: طلایه (the noun ‘vanguard/forefront’) is the head noun; پراگند is a postpositive adjective/participial adjective meaning ‘scattered’ modifying طلایه. بر گرد دشت is a prepositional phrase: بر (poetic preposition) + گرد (here ‘around’) + دشت (‘plain’), together ‘around the plain.’ Word order is noun + adjective then an adverbial prepositional phrase.
segments.s232-g138: چو begins a temporal clause meaning ‘when/as.’ درنگی is a verbal noun meaning ‘a pause’ and functions as the time-expression; شب اندر گذشت is the matrix clause ‘the night passed by’ with گذشت (past of گذر) as the main verb at the end. زنگی here is unclear/ambiguous in function (see senses); it appears as an epithet attached to درنگی (a descriptive word forming a tight phrase 'زنگی درنگی').
segments.s232-g139: پدید آمد is a light/compound verb meaning ‘appeared/rose up’ (پدید + آمد). آن خنجر تابناک is the subject phrase following the verb in this poetic order: آن (‘that/the’) + خنجر (‘dagger’) + تابناک (postpositive adjective ‘shining’).
segments.s232-g140: بکردار is an archaic/poetic particle/compound (بِ + کردار) here meaning ‘in the manner/appearance of’ and qualifies the following phrase; یاقوت (used adjectivally) + شد (‘became’) forms the predicate ‘became ruby(-like),’ and روی خاک (‘on the ground’) specifies the locus. The verb شد is perfective/past ‘became.’
segments.s232-g141: تبیره is the noun in subject position (here best read as ‘announcement/explanation’ in a literary sense); برآمد is the past verb ‘arose/issued forth.’ ز + پرده‌سرای is a poetic prepositional phrase ‘from the pavilion/stage’ showing origin. The verb–subject order (برآمد تبیره) is formal/literary.
segments.s232-g142: برفتند is a past/plural verb ‘they went/proceeded/advanced’; گردان لشکر is the nominative subject phrase ‘the army divisions/units’; ز جای is an ablative adverbial ‘from their place/from their posts.’ Word order puts the verb before its subject for poetic effect.
segments.s232-g143: چنین is an adverb/adverbial particle ‘thus/so.’ گفت here functions as the verb ‘said’ (the lemma list gave noun senses, so the verbal use is the standard literary past). رستم is the speaker in the nominative; بگردنکشان is an indirect-object/dative phrase ‘to the haughty/proud,’ formed with the prepositional b- prefix.
segments.s232-g144: که introduces the content clause (‘that…’). جایی نیامد is a negative clause ‘no place/sign came’ → here idiomatically ‘no sign came’; ز پیران نشان is a genitive/agentive phrase ‘a sign/from the elders’ that supplies the source of the (non-)appearance. نیامد is past negative ‘did not come.’
segments.s232-g249: This short adjectival phrase is a string of epithets modifying the person being praised. بکام دل is an inverted possessive-like phrase meaning “pleasing to the heart” (بکام + دل), with the possessed noun دل following بکام; شاد is a coordinating adjective “happy/joyful.” بادی و راد are two more epithets joined with و; in classical poetry such epithets can be terse and coordinate after the initial phrase.
segments.s232-g250: بدین رزم is a prepositional/locative phrase meaning “in/with this battle” (بـ- + this/این understood in the compound), followed by the verb دادی (past tense) 'gave'. چو introduces a concessive/temporal clause here meaning “when/as” or “just as,” and بایست داد is a phrase meaning “what ought to be given” or literally “ought/good to give” (بایست = impersonal/modal 'it should/was fitting', داد = ‘gave’ or ‘what is due’). The overall structure: [epithets], + [locative phrase] + past verb + subordinate clause introduced by چو describing the fittingness/time of the giving.
segments.s232-g251: This is a simple past clause: تهمتن (proper name/epithet) is the subject, فرستاده‌ای را is the (indefinite) object marked by را, and بجست is the past-tense verb 'sought' (archaic poetic past form). The object is formed from the participial/nominal sense of 'sent one' (a messenger).
segments.s232-g252: که introduces a relative/subordinate clause modifying 'a messenger' (i.e. 'a messenger who...'). با شاه گستاخ باشد is a subjunctive/hortative clause where باشد is the classical subjunctive/optative of بودن, here meaning 'would be' or 'might be' insolent; گستاخ modifies the messenger and با شاه = 'toward/with the king.' نخســت is an adverb 'first/at first' placed after the verb (typical poetic word order).
segments.s232-g253: فریبرز is the direct object (or the person chosen) and کاوس را here indicates the role/purpose (literally 'Fariborz he chose for Kāvus'); را after کاوس marks Kāvus as the reference/patient of the action in this phrasing (poetic object/topic marking). برگزید is the past tense verb 'chose/appointed.' The usual SOV order is observed but with topicalization common in poetry.
segments.s232-g254: که introduces the explanatory clause ('because' / 'for that'), with با شاه نزدیکی = 'closeness to the king' as the nominal phrase, او را the patient/emphatic pronoun 'him' (marked by را), and سزید the past verb meaning 'befitted/was appropriate for' (literally 'it was fitting/deserved him'). The clause gives the reason why Fariborz was chosen.
segments.s232-g255: چنین گفت is a conventional formula meaning 'thus he said' (چنین = 'thus/so', گفت here functions as the verbal past 'said' in poetic diction although the listed lemma senses are nominal; in context it is a reporting verb). کای is a vocative particle 'O' used before names/titles; نیک‌پی نامدار is a compound epithet 'Nikipi (well-born) renowned' — two adjectives/names in apposition addressing the addressee.
segments.s232-g256: This is a parallel noun phrase giving two attributes: هم ... و هم ... is the standard correlative 'both ... and ...'; از تخم شاهی = 'of royal seed/descent' (تخم = 'seed/origin'), and شهریار = 'sovereign/king.' Word order is canonical: preposition از before تخم, then شـاهی qualifying it; the second هم reiterates the parallelism.
segments.s232-g89: 'ز مردی نپیچید هرگز دلم' is arranged in a deliberately inverted, classical order: ز (from/of) + مردی (mardī) + نپیچید (did not turn) + هرگز (never) + دل + م (my). نپیچید is the past negative of پیچیدن meaning 'did not turn/waver'; دل + م is the noun with the first‑person enclitic ('my heart'). The whole segment means 'my heart never turned away from manliness.'
segments.s232-g90: 'نگفتم که از آرزو بگسلم' literally 'I did not say that I should sever from desire.' نگفتم is the past negative 'I did not say'; که introduces a clause ('that'); از آرزو = 'from/of desire'; بگسلم is a present/subjunctive form with ب‑ prefix meaning 'that I should break/sever (myself) away' (here used in the sense 'to separate oneself from something'). The clause reports that the speaker never declared he would renounce longing.
segments.s232-g91: 'جز آن دم که دیدم ز کاموس جنگ' = 'except that moment when I saw ...' جز آن دم که is a set phrase 'except that moment when'; دیدم is the simple past 'I saw'; ز here is the poetic/archaic preposition 'from/of' (often used like از); کاموس appears as a proper name (an individual) and جنگ follows as an epithet meaning 'of war/warrior' — together forming a name or title ('Kāmos the warlike' / 'Kāmos the warrior').
segments.s232-g92: 'دلم گشت یکباره زین کینه تنگ' = 'my heart became, all at once, tight with this grudge.' دلم (دل + م) = 'my heart'; گشت is the past of گشتن used as 'became/turned (into)'; یکباره = 'suddenly/at once'; زین is an archaic/poetic contraction equivalent to 'از این' ('from this' / 'by this'); کینه تنگ = 'narrow/close hatred' i.e. a constricting grudge. The clause describes a sudden inward change of feeling.
segments.s232-g93: 'کنون‌گر همه پیش یزدان پاک' = 'if now all of us before the pure God...' کنون‌گر here combines کنون ('now') with the conditional گر ('if') to mean 'if now'; همه = 'all/everyone'; پیش یزدان پاک = 'in the presence of (or before) the pure God' (پیش = before/in presence of; یزدان پاک = 'pure God'). This sets a conditional/hypothetical frame.
segments.s232-g94: 'بغلتیم با درد یک‌یک بخاک' — بغلتیم is a first‑person‑plural optative/subjunctive form meaning 'let us be enveloped/let us embrace/fall into' (poetically used for 'let us sink into' or 'let us be buried'); با درد = 'with pain/sorrow'; یک‌یک = 'one by one'; بخاک = 'to the earth / into the dust'. Together the clause expresses an exhortation or resignation: if we stand before God, let us each go down to the ground in sorrow.
segments.s232-g95: 'سزاوار باشد که او داد زور' is a classical phrase meaning 'it is fitting (or may it be fit) that he give strength.' سزاوار باشد is a conventional clause meaning 'it would be proper/worthy that...'; که introduces the subordinate clause; او is 'he'; داد here functions as 'give' (a verb) and زور = 'strength/might.' The construction expresses a wish or deeming something appropriate.
