segments.s232-g96: 'بلند اختر و بخش کیوان و هور' lists the things to be bestowed: بلند اختر = 'lofty/tall star' (a poetic epithet); و بخش = 'and the portion/gift'; کیوان = Kayvān (literally the planet Saturn, here a celestial name); و هور = 'and Hūr' (here meaning splendour/light or a proper name). The items form a poetic catalogue of divine gifts.
segments.s232-g33: This is a compound subject followed by the past-tense verb گفت ('said'). بنستیهن is an archaic/proper-name form; گرد here appears as part of the name/epithet (not a preposition) and is therefore treated as part of the subject. The coordinating و links the two names, and گفت comes at the end as the finite past verb, following classical Persian subject–verb order.
segments.s232-g34: که is the complementizer 'that' introducing the content of what was said. شمشیر و نیزه are the two coordinated objects ('sword and spear'). بباید is a modal/predicate meaning 'must/ought' (classical Persian present/future-modal form) and is followed by the verb نهفت (infinitive/old subjunctive of نهفتن 'to hide'), so the whole subordinate clause means 'that the sword and spear must be hidden/put away.'
segments.s232-g35: نگونسار is an adjective meaning 'mournful/woeful' placed before کرد ('made/did'); this is a causative/resultive construction: کرد makes نگونسار apply to the following noun. آن درفش سیاه is the noun phrase 'that black banner' (demonstrative + noun + color), and the verb کرد ties the adjective to the banner: 'they made that black banner mournful.'
segments.s232-g36: برفتند is the past plural 'they went off/they departed.' پویان is a participial/adjectival/epithet meaning 'moving/active' describing the subject; it follows the verb as a circumstantial modifier. به + (بیراه و راه) is a prepositional phrase 'to / along off‑road and road' (بیراه here = 'off the road/astray'), the two nouns joined by و in parallel.
segments.s232-g37: همه 'all' or 'the whole' modifies میمنه 'right side/flank' to give 'the whole right flank.' گیو is the proper-name subject (Giv). تاراج کرد is a verbal compound: تاراج 'plunder' as a verbal noun + کرد 'did/made' giving the past action 'plundered' or 'pillage was carried out by him.' Word order is modifier + flank, then subject, then verb.
segments.s232-g38: در و دشت ('town/gate and plain') is a coordinated object meaning 'towns and plains' (در here = gate/town rather than the preposition). چون is a comparative/conjunctive 'as/like' introducing the manner; پر 'full (of)' modifies دراج ('riders/horsemen'), and کرد completes the verbal action. The clause describes the manner of pillaging: he plundered town and plain 'as (if) full of horsemen' or more freely 'as he rode.'
segments.s232-g39: بجست is a past verb here meaning 'he searched/sought' (it can also mean 'leapt' elsewhere, but context shows seeking/scouting). از introduces the source/part 'from the left of the army and the right hand' (چپ لشکر = left flank of the army; دست راست = right hand/side). The coordinated phrase uses و between the two loci.
segments.s232-g40: بدان here functions as 'to find out/learn' (ب- prefix + دان 'know'); it expresses the action aimed at by the following purpose clause. تا is the purpose marker 'so that / in order that,' and بداند is the subjunctive/present-tense third‑person form 'he may know.' که introduces the embedded clause 'that the elders where‑are,' with پیران 'elders' as the subject and کجاست (کجا + ست) 'where is' as the copular question‑word clause. The whole phrase: '...so that he might learn where the elders are.'
segments.s232-g81: 'که' is a relative/complementizer here ('that' / 'which'); 'بودند' is the past plural of 'to be' ('they were'); 'هر یک' = 'each one' (the determiner 'هر' + pronoun 'یک' implied); 'چو' is a simile particle meaning 'like'; 'کوهی بلند' is a noun phrase 'a high/towering mountain' (noun + adjective). Word order is typical poetic inversion but reads literally 'that they were each like a tall mountain.'
segments.s232-g82: The locative phrase 'بزیر اندرون' stacks two prepositional/locative elements ('beneath' + 'inside/within') and the verb 'بود' is omitted (understood) after it; 'ژنده' is an epithet meaning 'tattered/ragged'; 'پیلی' is an epithet here 'elephantine' (forming a compound epithet with ژنده); 'نژند' is a final qualifying adjective 'base/worthless'. In classical poetry adjectives and epithets can appear in sequence with the copula dropped.
segments.s232-g83: 'بدل گفتم' = 'I said in return / I replied' (بدل as 'in return' adverbial, followed by the 1st-person past 'گفتم'); 'آمد زمانم بسر' is an inverted clause: literal order is 'زمانم بسر آمد' = 'my time came to an end' (آمد = 'came', زمانم = 'my time', بسر = 'to completion/pass' used adverbially). The inversion of verb and its complements is common in verse.
segments.s232-g84: 'که' introduces the subordinate clause; 'تا' here marks purpose/aim ('so that' / 'in order that'); 'من' is the first-person subject; 'ببستم' is the 1st-person form of 'بستن' used as a jussive/intentional action ('that I gird/tie'); 'بمردی' is an adverbial phrase 'manfully, in manhood'; 'کمر' is the object 'waist / girdle' (idiomatically 'gird one's loins'). The clause expresses purpose/resolve and uses poetic word order (verb before object).
segments.s232-g85: 'ازین بیش' = 'beyond these / greater than these' (از + این with poetic contraction/ordering; بیش = 'more'); 'مردان' here means 'men' in the heroic sense ('heroes'); 'و' is the coordinating conjunction 'and'; 'زین بیش ساز' parallels the earlier phrase and means 'and devices/means greater than these' ('زین' is a poetic form related to 'این' and 'ساز' = 'device/means' here, not necessarily musical). The phrase lists two categories being praised (men and their gear/devices).
segments.s232-g86: 'ندیدم' is the negated past 'I did not see'; 'بجایی' functions as a locative adverb 'in any place / elsewhere' (poetic usage); 'بسال دراز' is a temporal phrase 'for long years' (بسال = 'in/for years', دراز = 'long'), so the whole clause means 'I have not seen anywhere, for long years...'. The negative verb precedes the locative/time phrases by poetic inversion.
segments.s232-g87: 'رسیدم' is the 1st-person past 'I arrived / I reached'; 'بدیوان' is a fused prepositional phrase 'به دیوان' = 'to the court' (the court/assembly being the destination); 'مازندران' is the place name (Mazandaran) functioning as a genitive/locative complement to 'دیوان' (the court of Mazandaran).
segments.s232-g88: 'شب تیره' is a simple noun + adjective 'a dark night'; the conjunction 'و' links this phrase to 'گرزهای گران' which is 'maces (گرزهای, plural with -های) heavy/valuable (گران)'. In context these coordinate the setting and the armaments present: 'on a dark night and (with) heavy maces.'
segments.s232-g161: بدین گونه is an adverbial phrase ('in this manner/way') placed at the front to set the scene. دشمن میان دو کوه is a noun phrase where دشمن (enemy) is followed by the prepositional locative میان دو کوه ('between two mountains'), i.e. standard Persian noun + locative modifier word order.
segments.s232-g162: سپه is the subject 'the host/army'. چون introduces a subordinate time/condition clause here (classical usage = 'when/if'); گریزد is a present-subjunctive/optative-form used in narrative to mean 'flees'. ز ما is a prepositional phrase 'from/away from us' and همگروه functions adverbially/predicatively ('together/alongside [us]').
segments.s232-g163: طلایه is the direct object/name of the unit 'vanguard'. نگفتم in context is functioning as a verb of speech/command ('I told/ordered'); که introduces the content clause. بیرون کنید is an imperative/subjunctive construction ('go out' or 'send out'), where بیرون is the adverb 'out' and کنید is the 2pl present/subjunctive/imperative-form of کردن used for commands.
segments.s232-g164: در و راغ is a coordinated noun phrase (two things joined by و); چون here means 'like/as' introducing a comparison: دشت و هامون کنید is a command using the verb کنید ('make' / 'render') taking دشت و هامون ('plain and hamun/wasteland') as the comparison-standard — i.e. 'make X like the plain and hamun'. The overall structure is coordinated objects + verb in imperative.
segments.s232-g165: شما is the 2pl subject 'you'. سر بآسایش is an idiomatic construction where سر + به+N (here بآسایش) means 'to give one's head/oneself over to N' — 'to devote oneself to ease'; خوابگاه is coordinated as another complement ('and to sleeping-quarters'). The order (possessor/subject + سر + prepositional phrase) is typical for this idiom.
segments.s232-g166: سپردید is the 2pl past tense 'you handed/entrusted (over)', used here transitively to mean 'you gave yourselves over'. و simply connects clauses. دشمن is the subject of the following verb; بسیچید is a past tense verb meaning 'they gathered/mustered' and راه is the object/goal 'the way/road' — together: 'the enemy gathered on/for the road' (i.e. blocked or took up position on the road).
segments.s232-g167: تن‌آسان is a compound adjective/noun 'one who enjoys bodily ease' (a person fond of ease) serving as subject. غم و رنج is a coordinated object ('sorrow and hardship') and بار آورد is a causative-perfect/simple past verb phrase meaning 'brought/cause to bear' (آورد is past of آوردن). The verb applies to the compound object: 'brought grief and hardship.'
segments.s232-g168: چو introduces a temporal/conditional clause ('when/if'). رنج آوری is a present/subjunctive-like form (second-person or generalizing usage) 'you/one bring(s) hardship' and the main clause uses گنج بار آورد with گنج (treasure) as object and بار آورد ('brought a load') as the main verb in the past; the line sets up a contrast: 'when hardship is brought, (he) brought a load of treasure.'
segments.s232-g73: ز at the start introduces the cause or source ('because of' / 'from') in a poetic register. The sequence names three persons (بهرام, گودرز, ریونیز); وز between the names is a poetic contraction/functioning as a conjunction ('and' = و + ز), joining گودرز and ریونیز.
segments.s232-g74: دلم is the possessor + possessee ('my heart'); تیر‌تر is a comparative/adjectival use of تیر meaning an arrow/shot (here 'arrow-like' or 'more shot/struck by arrows'); گشت is the past/resultative verb 'became'. برسان is an imperative/verb 'bring/deliver' or a causative form 'make reach'; شیز is a vocative or name being addressed. Grammatically the clause states a change of state ('my heart became ...') and immediately issues an address/command.
segments.s232-g75: از ایران is a simple prepositional phrase 'from Iran'. همی is a poetic/progressive particle (here marking continued or immediate action). تاختم is a first‑person past verb 'I charged/rode swiftly' (تاختن), and تیزچنگ is an accompanying name or epithet (either the horse's name or an epithet 'Sharp‑claw').
segments.s232-g76: زمانی here means 'for a time' / 'for even a moment' (temporal adverb). بجایی is an adverbial phrase 'in a timely/appropriate place' (بجا = timely/appropriate). نکردم is the negated past first‑person 'I did not do', and درنگ is the noun 'pause, delay' which is the object of نکردم. The whole clause means the speaker did not pause at the proper time/place.
segments.s232-g77: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. چشمم is 'my eye' or 'my sight' (subject). برآمد is a past verb 'rose/turned up/appeared'. بخاقان is a prepositional phrase of direction or address toward the khaghan/court (poetic compound) — its exact lexical sense is irregular/archaic here — and چین is likely 'fold' or 'folded array' (a poetic image). Together they describe the gaze turning toward the ruler's fold/assemblage.
segments.s232-g78: بران is a deictic adverb 'upon that / thereon'. نامداران is an adjectival plural 'the renowned ones'; و links the coordinated noun مردان 'men' (here 'warriors' / 'heroes'). کین is a postpositive noun modifying مردان ('men of vengeance' = hostile, wrathful men). The phrase lists who are gathered/seen.
segments.s232-g79: بویژه is 'especially'. بکاموس is an adverbial/phrase meaning 'to one's liking' or 'pleasingly' (here 'especially pleasing'). و links the next nouns: آن فر 'that (royal) glory' and برز 'exaltation/height'; the clause emphasizes these qualities as particularly delightful or notable.
segments.s232-g80: بران again 'upon that / thereon' introduces the list of features on which the glory is displayed. یال 'mane' (metaphorical crest of a hero/horse), و آن شاخ 'and that horn', و آن دست 'and that hand', و گرز 'and mace' — a series of nouns coordinated to enumerate the attributes that carry the glory/exaltation.
segments.s232-g9: پیاده is an adverbial/adjunct placed before the verb meaning 'on foot'. همی is the archaic/progressive adverbial marker (literary), here giving a continuous or habitual sense to the verb راند (3rd‑person past/simple perfect 'drove/rode/marched'). تا introduces an extent/limit ('until/as far as') and governs the following noun phrase رود شهد, where رود 'river' is modified by شهد 'honey' (classical juxtaposition instead of an explicit ezāfe).
segments.s232-g10: This is a standard negative list using repeated نه ... و نه ... meaning 'neither ... nor ...'. The line is elliptical (no verb); the nouns (پیل, تاج, تخت, مهد) are listed as the things that are absent. The coordinating و simply links the items.
segments.s232-g11: چنینست is a fixed, emphatic predicate meaning 'it is thus' or 'such is'. رسم (custom/practice) forms a genitive construction with سرای فریب — 'رِسمِ سرایِ فریب' — i.e. 'the practice/custom of the house/realm of deception'. In classical Persian the ezāfe linking is often implied in poetry, so the three words form one noun phrase serving as the subject/complement of چنینست.
segments.s232-g12: گهی ... و گهی ... is a correlative pair meaning 'sometimes ... and sometimes ...'. Each phrase is built with the preposition بر ('on/upon') + noun: بر فراز 'on the summit/above' and بر نشیب 'on the descent/below'. The structure contrasts high and low states of that deceptive practice.
segments.s232-g13: چنین بود is the main clause 'it was thus'. تا introduces a temporal clause meaning 'as long as' or 'while', and the following بود is the verb of that subordinate clause ('there was' / 'existed'). گردان modifies سپهر: گردان سپهر is a poetic adjective+noun sequence meaning 'the turning/revolving sky/heavens'. The doubled بود is a classical stylistic repetition (main clause + temporal subordinate).
segments.s232-g14: This is a paired, contrastive listing introduced by گهی meaning 'sometimes'. The first member 'گهی جنگ و زهرست' reads as 'sometimes [there is] war, and it is poison' — زهرست is a predicative formation ('is poison'). The second member 'و گه نوش و مهر' = 'and sometimes [there is] drink/delight and love' (نوش here conveys 'drink/delight'). The verb 'is/was' is elided in the coordinate phrases.
segments.s232-g15: ازان پس is a temporal adverbial 'after that / thereafter'. بگرز is a 3rd‑person verb meaning 'turned/returned' (classical form); it takes گران دست ('mighty/heavy hand') as its subject (an adjective + noun sequence), and برد here functions as a noun ('victory' or 'gain') that the verb affects — so the clause means roughly 'thereafter the mighty hand turned/reversed victory'.
segments.s232-g16: بزرگش is بزرگ + possessive suffix ش 'his' = 'his greatness'. همان و همان is a doubled phrase for emphasis 'the same and the same' (= unchanged). بود is the copula 'was', and خرد ('wisdom') appears as a parallel predicate: the meaning is 'his greatness was the same, and his wisdom was the same too' — the syntax is somewhat elliptical, typical of epic verse.
segments.s232-g113: کنون is a fronted temporal adverb meaning “now.” می‌گساریم is a first-person plural verb with the present/continuous prefix می‑; in older/poetic Persian this verb conveys the ongoing action “we (will) carouse/revel” (here translated as a sustained action continuing up to the time specified). تا نیم‌شب is a temporal complement ‘until midnight’ that follows the verb and marks the endpoint of the action.
segments.s232-g114: بیاد بزرگان is a prepositional/phrastic phrase ‘in the memory (or for the remembrance) of the great ones.’ گشاییم لب is a verb phrase: گشاییم is a first‑person plural form of گشودن (here with an optative/performative flavor — ‘let us open’ / ‘we open’), and لب (‘lip’) is the direct object; the idiom گشودنِ لب = ‘to open one’s lips’ = to speak or sing.
segments.s232-g115: سزد‌گر is an archaic/poetic conditional particle meaning ‘if it be fitting’ (literally ‘one who deems fit’ used here as a concessive/conditional). دل is the affected noun ‘heart’; اندر is the preposition ‘in/within’; سرای (from the root سرا/سرود) here functions as the verbal/noun idea ‘to compose/sing’ or ‘the place of composing’ and combines with سپنج (‘sponge’) as a compound image (‘the sponge-house’ — a poetic location). Altogether this is a conditional clause: ‘If it pleases the heart to compose in the sponge-house…’
segments.s232-g116: نداریم is the present first‑person plural negative ‘we do not have/possess.’ چندین is a quantifier ‘several/many a.’ بدرد here is used adjectivally/nominally with the sense ‘of use / useful (things)’ (i.e. ‘useful items’), and و برنج adds ‘and rice’ as another thing we lack. The word order places the negated verb before the quantified objects — standard Persian negation with direct objects following the verb.
segments.s232-g117: بزرگان is the subject ‘the great ones / elders.’ برو here functions adverbially/particle-like (literally from ‘go’) meaning roughly ‘thereupon/then’ in this narrative use. خواندند is a past‑tense third‑person plural verb ‘they cried/sang/uttered,’ and آفرین is an interjection/acclamation ‘bravo’ or ‘well said.’ The clause reports the elders’ exclamation.
segments.s232-g118: که introduces a subordinate/explanatory clause ‘that/for (they said)…’. بی‌تو is the prepositional phrase ‘without you.’ مبادا is an optative/negative wishing particle meaning ‘may it not be’ or ‘let there not be’; it expresses a wish that the following not happen. کلاه و نگین are the coordinated nouns ‘cap (hat) and gem (jewel).’ Together the clause gives the content of the elders’ acclamation: ‘May there be no cap or jewel without you.’
segments.s232-g119: کسی را is a noun with the object/emphatic marker را (here introducing the person under comment). که begins a relative clause ‘who/that.’ چون is the comparative/simile particle ‘like/as’; پیلتن is a place-name (proper noun). کهترست is an archaic present‑tense predicate ‘is inferior / is lowly’ with the older ‑ست ending. The whole segment sets up a relative comparison: ‘a person who, like Piltan, is lowly…’
segments.s232-g120: ز is the poetic preposition ‘from/of.’ گرودن here denotes ‘approach / coming’ (a verbal noun or event), and گردان is ‘troop / host’ (a collective). سرش is سر + enclitic ش ‘his head.’ برترست is the present‑tense copular predicate ‘is higher / superior.’ Syntactically the clause compares the person’s head with the approaching/revolving host: ‘his head stands higher than the revolving troop (of Gurudan).’
